data.title: Has Ubiquiti Run Too Far After Soaring Over 100% in 2025?
data.description:
If you have ever wondered whether it is time to jump on the Ubiquiti train or maybe take some profits, you are not alone. Ubiquiti’s stock has been on a tear over the past year, vaulting up by an eye-popping 185.7% in just twelve months, and a jaw-dropping 303.2% over the past five years. Even if you only caught the year-to-date run, you would still be looking at a 112.1% gain. That kind of growth catches investors’ attention for good reason.

But let’s not ignore the bumps along the way. The past week saw the stock dip by 5.2%, hinting that sentiment could be shifting, or maybe just taking a breather after its latest surge. Over the last month, though, Ubiquiti is still up a solid 9.0%. These swings do not happen in isolation. Recent developments in the company’s product portfolio and network solutions have kept Ubiquiti in the headlines, making investors reassess its growth prospects and risk profile.

With so much movement, what are investors supposed to do now? Determining whether Ubiquiti is undervalued, fairly priced, or heading for a reality check is a major challenge. On the surface, our current valuation checks rate Ubiquiti a 1 out of 6 for being undervalued, suggesting limited immediate bargain potential. Still, numbers do not always tell the full story. Let’s break down the major valuation methods in the sections ahead, and stay tuned, because at the end I will share a smarter way to think about Ubiquiti’s true value.

Ubiquiti scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Ubiquiti Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company's intrinsic value by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today's dollars. The idea is simple: what is the present value of all the cash Ubiquiti will realistically generate for its shareholders in the years ahead?

Currently, Ubiquiti reports trailing twelve-month Free Cash Flow of $621.9 million. Analysts expect this figure to grow at a healthy pace, with forecasts indicating $693.1 million by 2026 and projects like $901.2 million by 2030, according to the model's assumptions. It is important to note that after about five years, most of these numbers are extrapolated by Simply Wall St based on current trends rather than direct analyst coverage. As a result, the later projections come with more uncertainty.

When applying these projections and discounting them to their present value, the DCF calculation arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of $268.17 per share. Compared with today’s market price, this model suggests Ubiquiti stock is currently trading at a 166.6% premium to its calculated value. In plain English, the stock looks significantly overvalued if we trust these DCF projections.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Ubiquiti.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Ubiquiti may be overvalued by 166.6%. Find undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Approach 2: Ubiquiti Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Ubiquiti, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is one of the most widely used ways to value a stock. It helps investors quickly compare how the market values a company’s earnings relative to other companies, both in the same sector and more broadly.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio depends on more than just profits. High-growth companies or those with less risk often command higher PE multiples, while slower-growing or riskier stocks usually trade at lower ones. For context, Ubiquiti’s current PE ratio is 60.8x. That stands well above both the broader Communications industry average at 30.7x and its peer group average of 73.2x.

However, simple comparisons can be misleading. That is why Simply Wall St. calculates a company-specific “Fair Ratio,” which blends factors like Ubiquiti’s growth outlook, profit margins, market cap, risks, and industry norms into a tailored benchmark. This approach provides a more nuanced picture compared to just looking at typical industry multiples. For Ubiquiti, the Fair Ratio is 40.4x, which is noticeably lower than its actual PE of 60.8x. This indicates that, given Ubiquiti’s fundamentals and risk profile, the stock is likely trading at a substantial premium to its fair value.

Result: OVERVALUED

PE ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Ubiquiti Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. A Narrative is your own story and perspective on a company, linking what you believe about its future to actual financial forecasts and an estimated fair value. Instead of just crunching numbers, Narratives connect Ubiquiti’s business outlook, projected revenue, margins, and earnings into a single, cohesive picture that you can easily adjust and understand.

Narratives are available for free within the Community page on Simply Wall St, making this approach accessible for any investor. Millions are already using Narratives to compare their fair value estimate with Ubiquiti’s current share price, helping decide when it is the right time to buy or sell. The best part is that these Narratives dynamically update as new news or earnings arrive, ensuring your analysis stays relevant.

For example, some Ubiquiti investors see substantial future growth and assign a fair value well above the market price, while others, more cautious, place their estimate much lower. This illustrates how Narratives reflect each person’s unique outlook in real time.

Over the past week, the stock nudged down by 0.8%, but zoom out just a bit and it is up 8.8% over the last month. Year-to-date, growth is a modest 2.0%. In the background, Extra Space Storage has been active in expanding its national portfolio through acquisitions and partnerships, aiming to solidify its position in the self-storage sector. This strategic activity means the company is regularly seeking efficiencies and growth, which investors take as a sign of long-term focus. Approach 1: Extra Space Storage Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates a company’s value by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today’s dollars. For Extra Space Storage, this approach uses adjusted funds from operations as the basis for estimating ongoing free cash generation.

Currently, Extra Space Storage reported free cash flow of $1.68 Billion. Analyst estimates suggest consistent growth, with projected annual free cash flow reaching approximately $2.02 Billion by 2029. While analysts offer detailed forecasts for the next five years, numbers beyond that are extrapolated and point toward continued, steady expansion as the company invests and scales its national footprint.

Based on this growth path, the DCF model calculates a fair value of $189.84 per share for Extra Space Storage. Since the current share price is $150.81, this implies the stock is trading at a 20.6% discount to its intrinsic valuation. Approach 2: Extra Space Storage Price vs Earnings

The Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is one of the most widely used metrics for valuing profitable companies like Extra Space Storage because it clearly reflects how much investors are willing to pay today for a dollar of current earnings. For companies with steady profitability, the PE ratio provides direct insight into the market’s growth expectations and its willingness to reward future earnings.

Growth expectations and risk both play a major part in deciding what constitutes a “normal” or “fair” PE ratio. Faster-growing or less risky companies typically warrant a higher PE multiple, while slower-growing or riskier companies may trade at a lower PE. Comparing PE ratios helps investors assess if a stock looks pricey or cheap relative to benchmarks, but context is crucial.

Extra Space Storage currently has a PE ratio of 32.8x, which is slightly above the peer average of 30.6x and considerably higher than the industry average of 17.8x. With Extra Space’s actual PE ratio (32.8x) only modestly below its Fair Ratio (35.0x), the stock appears to be trading close to fair value on this measure.

Looking ahead, analysts expect earnings to grow at a 7.4% rate per year, well behind the US market’s 15.5% average, and revenue growth to track at 4.4% annually. With margins still healthy and a reputation for quality earnings, the results highlight a steady but unspectacular performance relative to broader market expectations.

See our full analysis for Honeywell International.

Next, we’ll put these earnings numbers head-to-head with the current market narrative to see whether the community’s expectations match the reality, or if there are surprises in the underlying story.

See what the community is saying about Honeywell International

Advertisement

Separation Costs Stretch Margin Recovery

  • Honeywell faces over $1.5 billion in one-time costs from its plan to separate into three public companies. These expenses may weigh down margin expansion efforts despite a recent net profit margin of 14.3%.
  • Bears argue these restructuring expenses and execution risks could pressure near-term earnings, even if profit margins are projected to climb to 16.9% in three years.
    • Expected separation costs, together with $500 million in possible tariff exposure, may counteract positive impacts from future margin improvement. This highlights their concern about short-term profitability.
    • Bears also point out that revenue growth forecasts of only 3.7% per year trail both the US market and consensus expectations, adding to skepticism over Honeywell’s plans to unlock value through its breakup.
    Bulls say execution hiccups could drag on results. See why they’re sounding the alarm. 🐻 Honeywell International Bear Case

Dividend Strength Stands Out in Sector

  • While Honeywell’s price-to-earnings ratio of 24.5x is well below the peer average of 28.7x, its combination of high-quality earnings and an attractive dividend helps differentiate it from industry rivals.
  • The consensus narrative notes that ongoing share repurchases and segment growth in areas such as LNG and data centers are set to support stable dividends and enhance long-term value.
    • Analysts expect the number of shares outstanding to decline by 2.36% annually over the next three years. This can further boost earnings per share and keep dividend growth on a steady trajectory.
    • Expansion into high-growth verticals and continued productivity improvements are positioned to reinforce Honeywell’s appeal for dividend-focused investors, offering resilience even as margin recovery takes time.

Valuation Trades at a Premium to Industrials

  • Despite offering better value than direct peers, Honeywell’s 24.5x earnings multiple is still nearly double the global industrials sector average of 12.7x. Its $220.67 share price remains above DCF fair value of $202.34.
  • According to the analysts’ consensus view, the share price is trailing the official price target of $249.40, but the gap between current price and sector multiples reflects a premium that demands delivery on margin guidance and strategic execution.
    • While the stock looks attractive versus peers, the premium against broader industrials and the modest growth trajectory raise the bar for future performance.
    • Any shortfall in executing the separation plan or realizing expected revenue gains could narrow that premium quickly, making valuation a central debate point for both bulls and bears.

data.title: Does MARA Holdings Offer Real Value After 9% Jump Fuelled by Digital Expansion?
data.description:
If you are standing at the crossroads wondering whether to buy, hold, or sell MARA Holdings stock, you are definitely not alone. The journey for MARA has been anything but dull, and the latest market moves have only added to the debate over what the company is really worth right now. Over the last month, shares have powered up by 9.0%, building on an 11.7% gain since the start of the year. While it’s true there was a slight dip of -1.8% in the past week, the stock’s upward march over the last five years—an increase of 789.8%—reminds us of the growth potential that can lurk beneath volatility.

Recent headlines have focused on MARA’s aggressive expansion into new digital infrastructure projects, bolstering optimism about its long-term outlook. Investors are also weighing management’s bold moves into global partnerships, which could reshape competitive dynamics for years to come. Although these developments add fresh intrigue, the big question really comes down to valuation: with so much already baked into the price, is MARA still undervalued?

Our valuation checks suggest there is still a lot to like. On our scorecard covering six major valuation criteria, MARA Holdings nails four, earning it a value score of 4. That is a strong showing, and it means the stock lines up as undervalued on most of the metrics that matter.

Next, let’s break down which valuation approaches support MARA, where it falls short, and explore a smarter way to judge whether this company truly deserves your investment dollars.

Why MARA Holdings is lagging behind its peers

Advertisement

Approach 1: MARA Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

The Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model works by estimating the future cash flows a company will generate and discounting those amounts back to today’s dollars. This gives investors a clear sense of what the business might really be worth, beyond just current market hype or volatility.

For MARA Holdings, analysts estimate last year's Free Cash Flow (FCF) at negative $1,706.7 Million, reflecting ambitious reinvestment and ongoing growth initiatives. Looking ahead, projections become more optimistic. By the end of 2026, FCF is forecast to reach $146.5 Million, and by 2035, simply extrapolated projections point to approximately $890.3 Million in annual FCF, all measured in US Dollars. While only the first five years are based on analysts' estimates, these longer-range numbers offer a sense of the potential trajectory if growth continues at similar rates.

Based on these DCF projections, MARA Holdings’ intrinsic value is calculated at $24.08 per share, implying the stock is trading at a 20.2% discount to its estimated fair value. This means that, despite some near-term volatility and sizable reinvestment costs, the current price still undervalues the company’s long-term cash-generating potential.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MARA Holdings.

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests MARA Holdings is undervalued by 20.2%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover more undervalued stocks.

Approach 2: MARA Holdings Price vs Earnings

For companies that are profitable, the Price-to-Earnings (PE) ratio is a widely trusted metric for valuation. It gives investors a direct sense of how much they are paying for each dollar of earnings, making it especially relevant when a business is generating positive net income like MARA Holdings.

A company’s PE ratio is shaped by growth expectations and risk. Fast-growing and lower-risk firms usually command higher PE ratios, while those with uncertain outlooks or industry headwinds tend to trade at lower multiples. This context makes it key to compare MARA’s PE with industry standards and similar companies.

MARA Holdings currently trades at a PE of 10.5x, which is significantly below both the software industry average of 33.6x and its direct peers at 64.0x. At first glance, this discount might look like a bargain. However, peer and industry multiples can be skewed by outliers or companies with different growth or risk profiles. A higher or lower PE does not always signal mispricing.

To get a clearer picture, Simply Wall St uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio” model that factors in elements like MARA’s earnings growth, margins, market cap, industry norms, and risk profile to set a more tailored benchmark. For MARA Holdings, the Fair Ratio is 2.6x, much lower than its current 10.5x. This means MARA is trading above the level justified by our analysis of its fundamentals.

Result: OVERVALUED

PE ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Discover companies where insiders are betting big on explosive growth.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your MARA Holdings Narrative

Earlier, we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let’s introduce you to Narratives, a modern, hands-on tool for smarter investing. A Narrative is simply your story about a company, tying your outlook and assumptions (like future revenue, margins, and fair value estimates) directly to a financial forecast and then to a calculated fair value. Narratives bridge the gap between the numbers and the real-world events or beliefs driving them, helping you see exactly how your opinions play out in a company’s price tag.

Available right on Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives empower millions of investors to map their perspective to a fair value and compare it to the latest share price. In addition, Narratives automatically update as new information comes in, from earnings to news, ensuring your analysis stays current. This gives investors a practical edge because by simply comparing Fair Value to Price, you can decide when to buy, hold, or sell based on your unique view.

For example, some users see MARA Holdings unlocking new recurring revenue through AI and renewable energy expansion, setting a fair value as high as $28.00, while others remain cautious about its bitcoin mining risks and estimate fair value closer to $18.00. These diverging Narratives show how interpreting the same company through your own lens can lead to very different investment decisions.

data.title: How Investors May Respond To Ivanhoe Electric (IE) Raising $150 Million for Santa Cruz Copper Project Expansion
data.description:
• Ivanhoe Electric announced and priced an underwritten public offering of 10,000,000 common shares at US$15.00 each, with gross proceeds of approximately US$150 million, intended to fund land payments and early-stage activities at its Santa Cruz Copper Project in Arizona.
  • This significant capital raise highlights the company's focus on expanding its copper portfolio and advancing key development and exploration initiatives.
  • We'll examine how the sizable equity issuance and capital deployment plans may influence Ivanhoe Electric's broader investment narrative.

Find companies with promising cash flow potential yet trading below their fair value.

What Is Ivanhoe Electric's Investment Narrative?

To believe in Ivanhoe Electric as a shareholder right now, you have to trust in the vision for North American copper and critical mineral development, and have confidence that early exploration work will eventually justify current valuations, despite the company’s ongoing losses and limited revenue. The latest US$150 million equity raise is a clear signal that management is prioritizing growth at key projects like the Santa Cruz Copper Project, with the capital largely earmarked to secure land and accelerate development. This development both supports the most important short-term catalyst, the progression of Santa Cruz, and, by reinforcing the balance sheet, could deflate liquidity worries, at least temporarily. However, the greater share count amplifies dilution risk and the continued lack of profitability means market patience will be tested if production doesn’t ramp up as anticipated. The equity issue is a meaningful and material inflection for both upside catalysts and core risks alike.

But there’s also the risk of continued losses, which investors should keep front of mind. According our valuation report, there's an indication that Ivanhoe Electric's share price might be on the expensive side.

Exploring Other Perspectives

Among the Simply Wall St Community, two members’ fair value estimates for Ivanhoe Electric range widely from US$0.29 to US$19.10 per share. While optimism exists about future project catalysts and copper demand, these wide valuation views reflect real uncertainty around execution and financial outcomes.
